sources.full: Travelers Guide to Thelemar - Arcadia Homebrew Content
Travelers Guide to Thelemar - Illrigger Content

variantrule.name: Choosing to Fail Saves
entries: A character can willfully fail Dexterity Saving Throw and Strength Saving Throw saving throws, willfully gain disadvantage on Wisdom Saving Throw, Intelligence Saving Throw, and Charisma Saving Throw saving throws, but cannot do anything about Constitution Saving Throw saves.

variantrule.name: Critical Success & Critical Failure
entries: {@b Natural 1}: On non-attack rolls, a -5 penalty is incurred. On Attack rolls, the attack misses.
{@b Natural 20}: On non-attack rolls, a +5 bonus is applied. On Attack rolls, the attack hits.

variantrule.name: Exhaustion on being Downed
entries: Being downed in combat now gives 1 point of exhaustion (See {@condition exhaustion|TGTT} for the new exhaustion rules), only after death saves are resolved - meaning if your character is downed, they still have a chance to stabilize.

variantrule.name: Attacking While Falling
entries: When you attack a creature while falling on it from above, you add {@dice 1d4} damage for every 10 feet you fell.

variantrule.name: Identifying Magic Items & Effects
entries: A magic item has an identification DC based on its rarity level, where common equals {@dc 10} and every level thereafter goes up by 5 (uncommon is {@dc 15} and so on). If the item is not magical but is subject to a magical effect (for example, the {@spell thaumaturgy} cantrip), the DC is {@dc 10} + spell level (a cantrip's spell level is 0).

When a character comes into possession of a magic item, they should try to examine the item to find out what it does. They (or any other character) can roll an Arcana check in addition to the examination.

The examination should reveal any information about the item that can be understood from physically interacting with it (shape, color, material, weight, smell, taste, temperature, inscriptions, and any general feel the item can give). For example, a +x weapon may feel lighter, and playing around with it may reveal that it is easier to hit with it. The examination should be performed by the player and described to the DM, and the DM should describe to the player their findings.

Assuming the check was successful, the character making the check is assumed to (either through previous knowledge or through study of the item) understand most properties of the item, except for the exact number of charges (a general range may still be given) and command word (assuming it was not alluded to in any way in the item itself). Curses cannot be deduced by the check unless the DM wants them to be known.
If the examination wasn't performed, or the nature of the item is very hard to deduce from physical examination (up to the DM's judgment), the Arcana check is done with disadvantage.
If the nature of the item is very obvious after the examination (again, up to the DM's judgment), the check is done with advantage.
As an optional rule, failure in the Arcana check may lead to the character gaining wrong information about the item.

variantrule.name: Level 4
entries: When a character reaches level 4, they gain both a Feat and Ability Score Improvement, instead of having to choose between the two.

entries.name: Anxiety
entries: {@b Premise}: A character with a tendency to anxiety-related issues must perform one hour of self-care appropriate to their character (meditation, mindfulness activities, or something else) every day. Missing a session will lead to gaining a point of anxiety. The effects of anxiety points stack, so having 2 points gives the effects of both 2 and 1.

entries.name: Points of Anxiety
rows: Either (flip a coin) {@b Impulsiveness} or {@b Focus Problems}
{@b Anxiety}, and either (flip a coin) {@b Shutdown} or {@b Aggressiveness}

entries.name: Anxiety Effects
entries: {@b Slight Edginess}: The character suffers disadvantages on their skill checks and feels on edge.
{@b Impulsiveness}: The character has disadvantage on Wisdom saves and starts making rash decisions.
{@b Focus Problems}: The character has disadvantage on Intelligence saves and has difficulty focusing on things.
{@b Anxiety}: The character has disadvantage on attack rolls and does not add their proficiency bonus to attacks.
{@b Shutdown}: The character has disadvantage on Charisma saves and becomes extremely meek.
{@b Aggressiveness}: The character has disadvantage on Charisma saves and becomes aggressive, often resorting to brawn and anger.
{@b Anxiety Points} are reduced by 1 for every hour of self-care performed.

entries.name: Phobias
entries: {@b Premise}: A character with a phobia will need to roll a Wisdom Saving Throw when coming into a situation that faces them with the source of their phobia.
{@b Failure}: Results in the {@condition frightened} condition.
{@b Success}: Allows the character to act normally, but requires maintaining {@status concentration}, and incurs disadvantage on Wisdom Saving Throw.
The effects last for one full minute, after which a new saving throw is required.
{@b Critical Failure}: The character is {@condition incapacitated|tgtt}.
{@b Critical Success}: The character does not suffer any need for concentration or disadvantage on saving throws.

Encountering the phobia in a surprising manner (e.g., turning a corner in the street and seeing a giant spider, as opposed to crawling in a dungeon looking for danger) may incur disadvantage on the Wisdom Saving Throw against it, up to the DMs judgment.
The character can benefit from the {@action Help|tgtt} action when rolling their saving throw if another character is nearby and helps in a reasonable fashion.

variantrule.name: Reading Books
entries: Reading speed is calculated as: ***(1 + Intelligence modifier * 2) * 30 pages per hour***
This applies regardless of the book's rarity or topic. Book reading time can be split across multiple days, but if 30 days or more pass without any progress, all progress is lost.
When finishing a book, a character either gains a new lore skill or improves an existing one, as detailed in [Lore Skills](#p29).

variantrule.name: Relationships with Deities
entries: In Thelemar, the gods play a crucial role in the life of mortals. The relationships that form between gods and their followers, and even gods and their denouncers, can have meaningful impact on any campaign.

entries.name: Gaining Favour
entries: (Note - This section is in part inspired by the rules in Mythic Odysseys of Theros)

A god’s favour is given sparingly and not without conditions. Typically, a god will require devotion and reverence from their followers to gain favour. Each follower must maintain a unique and close relationship with their god to receive these benefits.
To actively seek a god's attention, a character must:

items: Pray at one of the god’s temples or in an acceptable place.*
Sacrifice to the god.
Carry or wear an item of faith, usually marked with a holy symbol.
Perform a small ceremony of initiation.

entries: After performing any of these, a character’s favour with the god rises by 1.

entries.name: Benefits of Favour
entries: Being in a god's favour confers certain powers and benefits, some more tangible than others.

{@b Prayer.} Favour factors into the chance a god will hear and heed the prayers of a character, though the way a prayer will be answered won't always be in direct manners (in contrast to features such as Divine Intervention). When a character prays to a god, roll a {@dice d100} and add +1 for every two points of favour that character has with that god. On a roll total of 100, the prayer is heard by the god, and is answered in a manner they see fit.
{@b Divine Boons.} At each Favour Tier detailed below, the god will grant a certain boon to the character that reached that tier. Specific boons are detailed in the gods section of this book. (TBD)

entries.name: Favour Tiers
entries: Favour is divided into five tiers, each with its own prerequisites, powers, prestige, and responsibilities.

entries.name: Devotee (Favour 3)
entries: {@b Acts to Achieve}: Following the faith, Praying, Carrying a holy symbol, Ritual.
{@b Requirements}: None.
{@b Responsibilities}: None.

entries.name: Votary (Favour 10)
entries: {@b Acts to Achieve}: Performing expected acts, Spreading the faith, Excelling in a domain of interest, Fighting the god’s enemies, Continued devotion (performing Devotee acts for long periods).
{@b Requirements}: Performing an expected act.
{@b Responsibilities}: None.

entries.name: Disciple (Favour 25)
entries: {@b Acts to Achieve}: Performing expected acts, Spreading the faith, Performing great deeds in the god’s name, Helping other followers of the god, Continued devotion (performing Devotee and Votary acts for long periods).
{@b Requirements}: Performing an expected act, Spreading the faith.
{@b Responsibilities}: None.

entries.name: Apostle (Favour 50)
entries: {@b Acts to Achieve}: Performing expected acts, Spreading the faith, Performing great deeds in the god’s name, Helping other followers of the god, Continued devotion.
{@b Requirements}: Performing an expected act, Spreading the faith, Performing a great deed.
{@b Responsibilities}: Spreading the faith.

entries.name: Champion (Favour 100)
entries: {@b Acts to Achieve}: Continued devotion, Excellence and distinction in the eyes of the god.
{@b Requirements}: Being chosen.
{@b Responsibilities}: Champion's duties.

entries.name: The Role of Champions
entries: Champions are a rare occurrence in Thelemar, and not every god has a champion at a given point in time. Champions are supposed to exemplify the god's intents and image upon the land, and carry out the most important and daring missions the god requires of them. They are the closest mortal servants a god has on Thelemar, and thus all have legendary status amongst their peers.

Achieving the role of champion is no easy feat, yet it is also quite different from gaining favour and rising in the ranks of the faith; some champions have been chosen despite having no previous favour with the god who chose them, while others spend their entire lives appeasing a god without ever being given the title.

In game terms, reaching 100 favour does not automatically grant the Champion Tier, nor is it really a prerequisite; gaining this tier is usually the culmination of a great character arc, with tumultuous trials and daring escapades. With that being said, gaining the Champion Tier does being the favour of a character directly to 100, conferring the same benefits of the previous tiers.

entries.name: Negative Favour: Malice
entries: Malice simulates negative relationships between gods and mortals. Players gain Malice by opposing a god’s interests, with the severity of actions determining Malice points (ranging from 1 to 100). Each god has unique banes (punishments) associated with Malice points.

entries.name: Divine Disfavour (Malice 5)
entries: {@b Banes}: Minor inconveniences like temporary discomfort or mild curses.

entries.name: Divine Displeasure (Malice 20)
entries: {@b Banes}: Hindered progress, reduced combat effectiveness, or long-lasting curses.

entries.name: Divine Ire (Malice 45)
entries: {@b Banes}: Ongoing curses, diseases, or debilitating conditions.

entries.name: Divine Fury (Malice 75)
entries: {@b Banes}: Life-threatening situations, powerful minions, or perilous trials.

entries.name: Divine Wrath (Malice 100)
entries: {@b Banes}: Facing divine champions, being hunted, or perilous trials.

entries.name: Atonement
entries: Players can atone for Malice by undertaking quests, performing acts of penance, or other redemptive actions aligned with the offended god’s interests to reduce Malice and its associated banes.

variantrule.name: Sleep Deprivation
entries: Characters require a minimum of 6 hours of sleep per long rest, unless a feature states otherwise. When a character fails to meet this requirement:

items: Make a Constitution Saving Throw after the first missed sleep period and every 12 hours thereafter.
The DC equals 5 + (cumulative hours of missed sleep).
On a failure, gain one level of {@condition exhaustion|tgtt}.
Update cumulative missed sleep hours every 24 hours.

entries: {@b Example}: Kay misses a full night's sleep (6 hours). In the morning, she rolls a Constitution Saving Throw ({@dc 11}). Twelve hours later, she rolls again ({@dc 11}). The next night, she sleeps for 1 hour. In the morning, she rolls a save ({@dc 16}, accounting for 11 cumulative hours of missed sleep). The next night, she sleeps for 1 hour. In the morning, she rolls a save ({@dc 16}, accounting for 11 cumulative hours of missed sleep).

variantrule.name: Sinking
entries: Creatures without a swimming speed must make Athletics checks to avoid sinking while submerged:

items: At the start of each round underwater, make an Athletics check ({@dc 15}, adjustable based on water conditions).
On a success, the creature doesn't sink and can move normally.
On a failure, the creature sinks and can only move downward.

entries: {@b Sinking Rates.} A creature sinks 5ft per round if they are Tiny or smaller, and 5 feet more for each size category above that (meaning 10 feet per round for Small creatures, 15 feet per round for Medium creatures, etc.)
{@b Armor Effects}:

items: No armor or light armor: No check required
Medium or heavy armor: Check required
Armor imposing disadvantage on Stealth: Disadvantage on sinking checks

entries: The DM may require checks or impose disadvantage for restrictive clothing or carrying heavy items.

variantrule.name: Unarmed Strikes & Natural Weapons
entries: Both unarmed strike and natural weapons are now considered *Finesse*.

variantrule.name: Linguistics Skill
entries: Languages evolve, mutate, and diverge. Fluent individuals may learn or pick up words in new languages, especially when surrounded by speakers of that language. This isn't covered in standard D&D 5e Language rules, but we can handle multi-lingual encounters in a more flexible, non-binary way.

entries.name: Rolling for Linguistics
entries: Ability modifier (Wisdom) + Proficiency modifier + Expertise modifier.
{@b Wisdom Modifier}: Wisdom is used because common sense and street smarts often help with understanding languages better than formal education.
{@b Proficiency Modifier}: Linguistics proficiency can be gained when leveling up, and some backgrounds may grant it for free.
{@b Expertise Modifier}: For each additional language a character speaks beyond their native one, they get a +1 bonus on Linguistics rolls.

entries.name: Setting Linguistics DCs
entries: Setting the DC is based on the difficulty level, with contextual modifiers like time spent, interruptions, or the familiarity of the language compared to the ones the PC already knows.

variantrule.name: Lore Skills
entries: Lore skills represent specialized information on narrow topics and are meant to give non-casters ways to be knowledgeable in areas of expertise without relying on default skills.
There is no default list of lore skills-they are created as needed by the GM and Player.

entries.name: Gaining Lore Skills
entries: Characters begin with 2 or 3 Lore Skills based on their background. The bonus for each skill does not include an attribute bonus, only the proficiency bonus.
Characters may gain new lore skills or improve existing ones by:

items: Reading a book.
Taking the {@feat Lore Mastery|TGTT} Feat, which provides 2 new Lore Skills or a +2 bonus to two existing Lore Skills.
Receiving tutoring.
Other methods the GM deems appropriate.

entries.name: List of Example Lore Skills
entries: Architecture, Art, Circus, Dungeoneering, Engineering, Farming, Fishing, Genealogy, Heraldry, Herbalism, Legal, Lore about a specific deity, Lore about a specific creature or narrow category of creatures, Lore of a specific plane other than the Material Plane, Lore about a specific settlement, Lore about a specific terrain, Lore of a type of food or drink, Mercantile, Midwifery, Mining, Nobility, Sailing, Scouting, Stabling, Tanning, Theater, Criminal Underworld, Warfare.

variantrule.name: Spell Availability
entries: In general, spells in Thelemar fall into one of 4 distinct categories:

items: {@b Common Spells} - Which can be learned at leveling up, found as loot, purchased at shops or given as prizes from NPCs and the like. These spells generally have no restrictions on their acquisition or usage.

{@b Uncommon Spells} - Spells that can be learned by classes only if mentioned by name as part of the class or subclass features (meaning, a class feature mentions "you gain X spell"). These spells can still be found as loot, purchased at shops, or given as prizes, but are harder to come by.
{@b Rare Spells} - these spells can only be found as loot or given as prizes. They are spells not known to the general public, and most if not all magical shops will not have copies of them for sale.

{@b Legendary Spells} - these spells can only be given as prizes to the players. They are the pinnacle of magic, known only to select few, and usually so unique and arcane that they can only be taught from one caster to the other, as mere writing cannot fully encompass their depth.

entries: In general, spells from outside sources (MCDM as an example) are considered uncommon or rare, while common spells correspond with all spells found in official rulebooks (save for specific exceptions, mentioned below).
Another note - some spells are marked as (special) - this denotes that every caster can cast them, given that they have access to the spell.

variantrule.name: Legal Status of Magic
entries: In Thelemar, magic is a part of life; as such, while some spells are useful for day to day life of average citizens, and others are mainly used for warfare and battles, few spells have come under the scrutiny of all rulers across the world. These spells are considered to be an affront to society, and their usage is limited by ruling bodies.

It is worth noting that cantrips are missing from the list, and with good reason - since most common cantrips are naturally occurring in the Children of The Empire, Thelemar's most common humanoids, banning cantrips would be an impossible task, and thus is not exercised - though having access to certain cantrips can have social repercussions.

Illegal Spells have a unique availability status - despite what might seem to be rational, they are all common. This us done to let players make their own choices regarding the usage of the spells and their own relationship with the law.

Below are the common illegal spells, divided into tiers of illegality; each tier comes with its own restrictions and punishment. With that being said, some kingdoms and areas will have specific rules regarding magic usage which might affect or change this list - consult your DM before making choices!

entries.name: Tier I
entries: In this tier, spells are considered dangerous, but not completely disruptive to society. Having used them or possessing them without proper registration will lead to a fine in most cases.

entries.name: Tier II
entries: In this tier, spells are considered disruptive to society, and using them might lead to capital punishment; possessing these spells without repercussion is possible with proper license for specific purposes (research, for example)

entries.name: Tier III
entries: In this tier, spells are consider dangerous and extreme, and both possessing and using them might lead to capital punishment.

entries.name: Tier IV
entries: In this tier, spells are considered evil. Using or possessing them will lead to capital punishment, and the killing of a person who have used or possesses these spell is not considered a criminal offense (meaning using them might lead to being attacked by people other than law enforcement without repercussions).

variantrule.name: Recognizing Spells
entries: It is very common that when a spell is cast, a caster will try to recognize the spell being cast. To facilitate this behavior and streamline its usage in Thelemar, the following rules are written:

items: To recognize a spell, players can make an Arcana check; This is a free action.
The DC for Spells in the characters spell list is 10 + modifiers, and for spells not in their spell list it is 15 + modifiers.
Modifiers are as follows - Add 1 for each spell level (cantrips add zero). Add 5 for each availability level above common (meaning +5 for uncommon, + 15 for legendary). Decrease 5 for Illegal Spells.

variantrule.name: Surprise
entries: If a combatant is {@condition undetected|TGTT} to all of its enemies in an encounter, they get advantage on their initiative roll.
Once before they become detected by any enemy, they may use a free action called a {@b Surprise Action} at any point in combat, which lets them do an activity that normally takes one action.
If the combatant loses the {@condition undetected|TGTT} condition before using their Surprise Action, they can't use it this combat.
The DM gets final say on when a combatant enjoys these benefits. The DM may rule that the combatant enjoys these benefits even if some enemies observe the combatant.

variantrule.name: Initiative Rage
entries: When rolling initiative, a {@class barbarian} can choose to activate their rage as part of the initiative roll (i.e, as a free action).

variantrule.name: Stamina
entries: Stamina is a resource used to power {@filter combat methods|combatmethods}. Your stamina pool equals twice your proficiency bonus, and you regain all spent stamina when you finish a short or long rest.

entries.name: Using Stamina
entries: When you use a combat method, you spend stamina points from your pool as indicated by the method. Some methods cost 1 point, while more powerful methods may cost 2 or more points.

entries.name: Alternative Sources
entries: {@b Monks} can use their {@class Monk|TGTT|focus points|focus} to pay for stamina costs in addition to their normal stamina pool.

{@b Paladins} can expend spell slots as a bonus action at the start of their turn to gain temporary stamina points that last until their next long rest. A 1st-level spell slot grants 2 stamina points, plus 2 more for each spell slot level above 1st.

variantrule.name: Method DC
entries: Some combat methods require a target to make a saving throw. Your method DC is calculated as follows:

entries.name: Method Save DC
entries.text: 8 + your proficiency bonus + your Strength or Dexterity modifier (your choice)

entries: {@b Spellcasters} can use their spellcasting DC instead of the standard method DC.
{@b Monks} gain a +1 bonus to their method DC, and can add their Wisdom modifier instead of Strength or Dexterity when calculating their DC
{@b Illriggers} can use their Interdict save DC instead of the standard method DC.

variantrule.name: Combat Stances
entries: Some combat methods are stances—sustained combat forms that provide ongoing benefits. A stance method is activated as a bonus action and remains active until you use a bonus action to end it, you activate a different stance, or you are {@condition incapacitated}.
You can only maintain one stance at a time. Stances do not require {@variantrule concentration|phb}, but maintaining a stance requires you to remain conscious and able to take actions.

variantrule.name: Combat Traditions
entries: Combat traditions are schools of martial technique, similar to schools of magic for spellcasters. Each tradition represents a distinct fighting philosophy with its own methods organized by {@variantrule Method Degrees|TGTT} (1st through 5th).
To learn a method, you must be proficient in its tradition. Your class determines which traditions you can learn, and you gain proficiency in traditions as specified by your class features.
Some methods are called {@variantrule Combat Stances|TGTT}, and have a sustained effect once activated. Others are single use activation.

entries.name: Available Traditions
entries: {@filter Adamant Mountain|combatmethods|tradition=Adamant Mountain} focuses on hardiness, might, and enduring your opponents' blows.
{@filter Arcane Knight|combatmethods|tradition=Arcane Knight} blends magic with martial prowess.
{@filter Beast Unity|combatmethods|tradition=Beast Unity} develops deep connections with animal companions.
{@filter Biting Zephyr|combatmethods|tradition=Biting Zephyr} masters ranged combat and thrown weapons.
{@filter Comedic Jabs|combatmethods|tradition=Comedic Jabs} uses humor and wit as weapons.
{@filter Eldritch Blackguard|combatmethods|tradition=Eldritch Blackguard} channels dark magic through martial techniques.
{@filter Gallant Heart|combatmethods|tradition=Gallant Heart} embodies chivalry and noble combat.
{@filter Mirror's Glint|combatmethods|tradition=Mirror's Glint} focuses on defense, parries, and reflection.
{@filter Mist and Shade|combatmethods|tradition=Mist and Shade} emphasizes deception, feinting, and mental games.
{@filter Rapid Current|combatmethods|tradition=Rapid Current} prizes speed, swift strikes, and mobility.
{@filter Razor's Edge|combatmethods|tradition=Razor's Edge} requires awareness, concentration, and discipline.
{@filter Sanguine Knot|combatmethods|tradition=Sanguine Knot} focuses on teamwork and fighting alongside allies.
{@filter Spirited Steed|combatmethods|tradition=Spirited Steed} masters mounted combat.
{@filter Tempered Iron|combatmethods|tradition=Tempered Iron} channels confidence, conviction, and zealous pursuit.
{@filter Tooth and Claw|combatmethods|tradition=Tooth and Claw} embraces animalistic, predatory combat.
{@filter Unending Wheel|combatmethods|tradition=Unending Wheel} embodies mastery, patience, and refined training.
{@filter Unerring Hawk|combatmethods|tradition=Unerring Hawk} perfects precision strikes and keen observation.

variantrule.name: Method Degrees
entries: Combat methods are organized into five degrees of power, similar to spell levels. As you gain levels in a class that grants combat methods, you gain access to higher degree methods.

Your class's Method Degree column determines the highest degree of method you can learn. When you learn a new method, you can choose any method of a degree you have access to from a tradition you are proficient in.

{@b Learning Higher Degree Methods:} You can learn methods of any degree up to your maximum. For example, a 12th-level {@class fighter} has access to 4th degree methods, but can still learn 1st, 2nd, or 3rd degree methods when gaining new methods known.

variantrule.name: Carrying Capacity
entries: Your size and Might modifier determine the maximum weight in pounds that you can carry, as shown in the Carrying Capacity (Might) table. The table also shows the maximum weight you can drag, lift, or push.
While dragging, lifting, or pushing weight in excess of the maximum weight you can carry, your Speed can be no more than 5 feet.

variantrule.name: High Jump
entries: When you make a High Jump, you leap vertically a number of feet equal to 3 + your Strength modifier if you moved at least 10 feet immediately before the jump; a standing high jump is half that distance. Each foot of the jump costs a foot of movement.
You can reach 1.5 × your height above the jump's apex (the height you jump plus half your height of arm extension).

For quick reference by Athletics modifier (A): Running = {@dice 2 + #$prompt_number:title=Enter Athletics Modifier$# × 0.5|2 + A × 0.5} ft.; Standing = {@dice 1 + #$prompt_number:title=Enter Athletics Modifier$# × 0.25|1 + A × 0.25} ft.

variantrule.name: Long Jump
entries: When you make a Long Jump, you leap horizontally up to your Strength score in feet if you moved at least 10 feet immediately before the jump; a standing long jump is half that distance. Each foot you jump costs a foot of movement.
If you land in difficult terrain, make a {@dc 10} Dexterity ({@skill Acrobatics|XPHB}) check or fall {@condition prone|TGTT}.

For quick reference by Athletics modifier (A): Running = {@dice 8 + #$prompt_number:title=Enter Athletics Modifier$#|8 + A} ft.; Standing = {@dice 4 + #$prompt_number:title=Enter Athletics Modifier$# × 0.5|4 + A × 0.5} ft.

variantrule.name: Item Utilization
entries: Items that either deal damage or heal upon being used, and require a bonus action to be used, can also be used as an action - in which case no roll is required, and the maximum number possible for the roll is taken.

variantrule.name: Tracking
entries: Tracking is an ancient art, a conversation between the hunter and the earth. While a novice might spot a muddy boot print, a master ranger reads the story of the land: the snapped twig, the disturbed dew, and the weight of the creature that passed. These guidelines expand on the tracking rules found on page 244 of the {@book Dungeon Master's Guide|DMG|244} and bring additional narrative cues to the trail.

entries.name: The Tracking Check
entries: To track a quarry, a character must make a Wisdom ({@skill Survival}) check. The base DC depends on the surface terrain and is further modified by time, environment, and the quarry’s precautions.
Choose the surface category that best represents the majority of the terrain, then apply any modifiers.

entries.caption: Tracking DCs
rows: Fresh snow, thick mud, wet sand
Dirt, grass, forest floor, dusty floor
Rocky ground, ice, bare wood floor
Scrubbed stone, flowing water, magic affected tiles

entries.name: Tracking Modifiers
entries: Adjust the DC or apply advantage/disadvantage whenever the trail’s circumstances suggest it.

items: {@b Crowd.} -1 DC for each creature beyond the first (maximum -5).
{@b Blood Trail.} -5 DC if the quarry is wounded or bleeding.
{@b Hiding.} If the quarry is actively obscuring its tracks, use the higher of the base DC or the quarry’s Dexterity ({@skill Stealth}) check.
{@b Age of Trail.} +5 DC for every 24 hours that pass after the trail is made.
{@b Weather.} Disadvantage if rain, snow, or strong wind has occurred since the quarry passed.
{@b Visibility.} Disadvantage in dim light or fog without darkvision.
{@b Disturbance.} +5 DC if the area has been crossed by other creatures.

entries.name: Reading the Signs
entries: Tracking isn’t binary; higher results reveal richer information. Compare the check result against the DC and note the margin.

entries.caption: Degrees of Success
rows: You cannot find the trail, or you lose it. Spend 10 minutes indoors or 1 hour outdoors searching before trying again.
{@b The Path.} You locate the trail and can follow it for a travel segment or 1 minute in combat, learning the general direction of travel.
{@b The Quarry.} You identify the creature type, exact numbers, and their travel pace (slow, normal, fast).
{@b The Story.} You determine the time of passage within 1 hour and identify the specific species or notable composition.
{@b The Unseen.} You spot minute details such as encumbrance, wounds, or riders, and recognize individual tracks you’ve seen before.

entries.name: Movement While Tracking
entries: Tracking demands focus, and travel speed changes what you perceive.

items: {@b Normal Pace.} You have disadvantage on the Survival check to track.
{@b Fast Pace.} You cannot track your target.

variantrule.name: The Dreamtime
entries: The Dreamtime is a plane much like the elemental planes or the astral plane, but it can be accessed with only dreams. It houses only one type of rare native beings (Nyuidj), yet is teeming with visitors who come there in their sleep.

entries.name: Strange Mirror
entries: The Dreamtime acts as a peculiar reflection of the real world, though it's impermanent in nature. Buildings manifest only if they've existed in the physical world long enough (usually a couple of months). Light appears ambient with no obvious source. People feel a constant feeling of unseen eyes watching them.

entries.name: Temporal Distortion
entries: Time within The Dreamtime flows differently than in the waking world. A person may be asleep for only an hour in the waking world, but may experience several hours in The Dreamtime, or vice versa.

entries.name: Fluid Reality and Power of Will
entries: Within The Dreamtime reality is malleable and shaped by the thoughts and emotions of those present. The key to mastering this realm lies in the constant awareness that it is a dream. If someone accepts events as reality, they risk becoming ensnared in the dream.

entries.name: Shared Dreamspace
entries: The Dreamtime is a shared dream, enabling individuals to meet and interact with one another within its surreal landscapes. Skilled Dreamwalkers can also invade others' regular dreams.

entries.name: Nexus for Souls
entries: Souls of the deceased can sometimes be found in the Dreamtime, and even interacted with, though this occurrence is highly unusual.

entries.name: Nyuidj, the Dream Children
entries: No one truly knows how these creatures came to be. Nyuidj are not a specific race as much as they are a phenomena, mortal creatures with high affinity to The Dreamtime that have spent a considerable amount of their existence in it. They are often Dreamwalkers and always possess psychic powers in the waking world.

variantrule.name: Dreamtime: Time Tracking
entries: When a person enters the dreamtime, the DM rolls on the Time Dilation table to determine how time will move for the person. This result lasts as long as the person is inside the dreamtime.
If two individuals meet or are close in the dreamtime, their time results morph to become closer to being 1:1 with real world passage of time.

entries.caption: Time Dilation
rows: 1 hour in Dreamtime = 10 minutes in real world
1 hour in Dreamtime = 20 minutes in real world
1 hour in Dreamtime = 30 minutes in real world
1 hour in Dreamtime = 1 hour in real world
1 hour in Dreamtime = 2 hours in real world
1 hour in Dreamtime = 3 hours in real world
1 hour in Dreamtime = 6 hours in real world
1 hour in Dreamtime = 8 hours in real world

variantrule.name: Dreamtime: Toll on the Mind
entries: While a person is in the dreamtime, every two (real world) hours spent inside it counts only as one towards their resting time. For example if a person enters the dreamtime and sleeps for 8 hours, upon leaving it and waking, their body would have rested only for 4 hours.

variantrule.name: Dreamtime: Real World Consequences
entries: Although The Dreamtime is a dream world, things that affect you there also affect you in the real world. If you are injured or killed in The Dreamtime, your injury or death is just as real as if it occurred in the real world. If you use abilities or spell slots, they are spent even if they stem from physical attributes.
Beneficial effects do not carry over the same way; you cannot rest or gain hp in the dreamtime while dreamwalking.

Some abilities refer to regular dreams; in these dreams there is no connection to the real world—your "real" self cannot be harmed within another's dream. The only thing you carry out of another person's dream is your memory of the dream events.

variantrule.name: Dreamtime: Ambient Change
entries: The dreamtime is both fixed in place, and constantly changing. Doors open and close, cutlery might move from the table to the cupboard, and piles of garbage might contain different items from moment to moment.

A special example is time keeping devices, such as clocks; these will always exhibit quirks when observed, such as clocks having too few or too many hands, running faster, being inconsistent with one another, running backwards and so on.

entries.caption: Ambient Changes
rows: Doorways open and close randomly around you.
Object flickers in and out of existence.
Furniture rearranges itself when not observed, even between blinks.
Colors of an item around you changes.
Sounds echo strangely and seem to come from everywhere at once.
Temperature varies wildly, from freezing cold to scorching heat.
Time appears to loop, causing déjà vu moments.
A random person dreams themselves into the area for several moments and disappears back into their dreams.

variantrule.name: Dreamtime: Consistency of Change
entries: The dreamtime is an ever-changing, ever-morphing landscape. Despite the fact that many abilities allow dreamers and the rare inhabitants to shape their surroundings (intentionally or unintentionally), changes are never consistent enough to stick over time.
When a dreamer leaves the dreamtime, the changes they created start to erode and disappear, such that the next time they visit, those changes are no longer in effect.
This rule is true in general, but some abilities (such as the Dreamorph ability) break it—in such a case, the rules of the ability supersede this rule.

variantrule.name: Dreamtime: Constant Effort
entries: In the dreamtime, losing your focus can spell disaster. Every 10 minutes spent inside the dreamtime, a creature must roll a concentration check, with {@dc 15}. On failure, their stray thoughts shape the dreamtime around them—roll {@dice 2d4} on the Dreamtime Consequences table for outcome. Effects last for 1 hour unless stated otherwise.

entries.caption: Dreamtime Consequences
rows: Dreamers become trapped in their own minds, unable to awaken. The DC to exit is 25, or 40 for dreamers using their own body. Such a roll can only be attempted once every hour.
Terrain changes unpredictably, creating hazardous conditions. Roll on the Hazardous Terrain Table.
Dreamers lose control of their dream appearance, changing form. Rolls of {@skill Insight} against a dreamer in this state have advantage.
Time dilation accelerates. The dreamer's time dilation rises by 2 levels.
Random objects manifest, potentially causing harm or confusion.
Dreamed enemies appear, attacking the dreamer. The enemies are a reflection of recent encounters.
Nightmarish creatures emerge from the dreamers' subconscious and attack them.

entries.caption: Hazardous Terrain
rows: {@b Corrosive Swamp}. A swamp rife with corrosive acids. At the start of a creature's turn it makes a {@dc 13} Dexterity ({@skill Acrobatics}) check. On a failure, it takes {@damage 2d6} acid damage.

{@b Sandstorm Desert}. A creature caught outside of shelter must make a {@dc 15} Constitution saving throw at the end of its turn or be {@condition blinded} for 1 round. Failure by 5 or more causes {@damage 2d4} slashing damage.
{@b Frozen Tundra}. Every 10 minutes, a creature makes a {@dc 13} Constitution saving throw or takes {@damage 2d4} cold damage. Failure by 5 or more causes one level of {@condition exhaustion|TGTT}.

{@b Volcanic Fields}. Every 100 feet of travel, roll {@dice 1d20}. On 15+, lava spouts within 20 feet. Creatures within 15 feet make a {@dc 15} Dexterity saving throw or take {@damage 5d10} fire damage.

variantrule.name: Gemstone Empowerment
entries: Empowered gemstones are created by a character proficient with {@item jeweler's tools|PHB} through a special crafting process. Once all requirements are met, the process of creating a gemstone is performed over a two hour period, which can be split into shorter sessions such as during a short rest.

At the end of the period, make a craft check. When you make this check, you don't benefit from any bonuses (such as Bardic Inspiration) except your own ability score and proficiency bonus, unless you benefited from the bonus for the entire duration of the period.
{@b Gemstone Empowerment bonus} = your proficiency bonus + your Charisma or Wisdom modifier (your choice)

On success, the gemstone is empowered successfully and becomes an empowered gemstone, otherwise the gem is muted and cannot be empowered anymore. The DC depends on the rarity of the gemstone, as shown in the Gem Empowerment table.

An empowered gemstone can then be affixed to a piece of equipment (armor, shield, or weapon as specified by the gemstone's description) by a character proficient with {@item jeweler's tools|PHB} over the course of a short rest. A gemstone can be removed and re-affixed to different equipment during a short rest.

condition.name: Exhaustion
entries: While you are subjected to the Exhausted Condition (known in older books as Exhaustion), you experience the following effects:

items: {@b Levels of Exhaustion}: This Condition is cumulative. Each time you receive it, you gain 1 level of exhaustion. You die if your exhaustion level exceeds 10.
{@b d20 Rolls Affected}: When you make a d20 Test, you subtract your exhaustion level from the d20 roll.
{@b Spell Save DCs Affected}: Subtract your exhaustion level from the Spell save DC of any Spell you cast.
{@b Ending the Condition}: Finishing a Long Rest removes 1 of your levels of exhaustion. When your exhaustion level reaches 0, you are no longer Exhausted.

condition.name: Incapacitated
entries: While you are Incapacitated, you experience the following effects:

items: {@b Inactive}: You can't take Actions or Reactions.
{@b No Concentration}: Your Concentration is broken.
{@b Speechless}: You can't speak.
{@b Surprised}: If you are Incapacitated when you roll Initiative, you have Disadvantage on the roll.

condition.name: Slowed
entries: While you are Slowed, you experience the following effects:

items: {@b Limited Movement}: You must spend 1 extra foot of movement for every foot you move using your Speed.
{@b Attacks Affected}: Attack rolls against you have Advantage.
{@b Dexterity Saves Affected}: You have Disadvantage on Dexterity Saving Throw.

condition.name: Hidden
entries: You are unseen. Your location might be deciphered through means other than direct sight (footprints, sound, smell, etc…)

items: You might be Hidden to some creatures and not hidden to others.
When you are heavily obscured or behind full cover, you are hidden.
When you are {@condition hidden|tgtt}, you gain advantage on {@action attack} rolls, and creatures targeting you gain disadvantage - see the rules for unseen attackers and targets.

Note: Though cover grants you the hidden condition, most attacks require you to peek out of cover to make them, making you lose the hidden condition before you make the attack, and therefore not getting advantage. See the {@action Hide|tgtt} action to learn how to use the Hidden condition granted from cover to enhance attacks.

condition.name: Invisible
entries: You are {@condition hidden|TGTT} to all creatures that rely on sight, unless they can see invisible creatures (through means like true-seeing, blind-sight, etc…)

condition.name: Undetected
entries: Your location is unknown. You must be {@condition hidden|TGTT} to be undetected.

items: You might be undetected by some creatures and detected by others.
Note: Being Undetected by all enemies before combat might grant you advantage on initiative. See the rules for Surprise.

condition.name: Petrified
entries: While you have the Petrified condition, you experience the following effects.

items: {@b Transformed.} You are transformed, along with any nonmagical items you are wearing and carrying, into a solid inanimate substance (usually stone). Your weight increases by a factor of ten, and you cease aging.
{@b Incapacitated.} You have the {@condition Incapacitated|TGTT} condition.
{@b Unaware.} You are aware of your surroundings but can't speak or move.
{@b Attacks Affected.} Attack rolls against you have Advantage.
{@b Saving Throws Affected.} You automatically fail Strength Saving Throw and Dexterity Saving Throw.
{@b Resistances.} You have Resistance to all damage.
{@b Poison Immunity.} You have Immunity to the {@condition Poisoned} condition. Any poison already in your system is suspended, not neutralized.

condition.name: Stunned
entries: While you have the Stunned condition, you experience the following effects.

items: {@b Incapacitated.} You have the {@condition Incapacitated|TGTT} condition.
{@b Can't Move.} You can't move.
{@b Speech Limited.} You can speak only falteringly.
{@b Saving Throws Affected.} You automatically fail Strength Saving Throw and Dexterity Saving Throw.
{@b Attacks Affected.} Attack rolls against you have Advantage.

condition.name: Dazed
entries: While Dazed, you experience the following effect:
{@b Limited Activity}: You can Move or take one Action on your turn, not both. You also can't take a Bonus Action or a Reaction.

condition.name: Choked
entries: When Choked, you experience the following effects:

items: {@b Tongue Tied}: You can speak only falteringly, hampering your ability to communicate properly.
{@b Short Breath}: You can hold your breath for half your maximum time.
{@b Cough}: When casting a spell that includes a verbal component, you must first roll a concentration check. On failure, the spell is disrupted.

condition.name: Grappled
entries: While you have the Grappled condition, you experience the following effects.

items: {@b Speed 0.} Your Speed is 0 and can't increase.
{@b Attacks Affected.} You have Disadvantage on attack rolls against any target other than the grappler.
{@b Movable.} The grappler can drag or carry you when it moves, but every foot of movement costs it 1 extra foot unless you are Tiny or two or more sizes smaller than it.
{@b Somatic Constraint.} When attempting to cast a spell with a somatic component while {@condition grappled|TGTT}, you must first roll a concentration check. On failure, the spell is disrupted.

condition.name: Restrained
entries: While you have the Restrained condition, you experience the following effects.

items: {@b Speed 0.} Your Speed is 0 and can't increase.
{@b Attacks Affected.} Attack rolls against you have Advantage, and your attack rolls have Disadvantage.
{@b Saving Throws Affected.} You have Disadvantage on Dexterity Saving Throw.
{@b Somatic Ban.} When attempting to cast a spell with a somatic component while restrained, the spell is automatically disrupted.

condition.name: Poisoned
entries: While you have the Poisoned condition, you experience the following effects.

items: {@b Ability Checks and Attacks Affected.} You have Disadvantage on attack rolls and ability checks.
{@b Concentration Affected.} You have Disadvantage on Constitution Saving Throws to maintain concentration.

condition.name: Frightened
entries: While you have the Frightened condition, you experience the following effects.

items: {@b Ability Checks and Attacks Affected.} You have Disadvantage on ability checks and attack rolls while the source of fear is within line of sight.
{@b Can’t Approach.} You can’t willingly move closer to the source of fear.
{@b Verbal Constraint.} When attempting to cast a spell with a verbal component while frightened, you must first roll a concentration check. On failure, the spell is disrupted.

condition.name: Prone
entries: While you have the Prone condition, you experience the following effects.

items: {@b Restricted Movement.} Your only movement options are to crawl or to spend an amount of movement equal to half your Speed (round down) to right yourself and thereby end the condition. If your Speed is 0, you can't right yourself.
{@b Attacks Affected.} You have Disadvantage on attack rolls. An attack roll against you has Advantage if the attacker is within 5 feet of you. Otherwise, that attack roll has Disadvantage.
{@b Concentration Disruption.} When you are knocked prone, you must roll a Concentration check if you are concentrating on a spell.

disease.name: Chameleon's Lament
entries: Chameleon's Lament, also called the Changeling Disease, is a rare autoimmune condition that affects Changelings exclusively. It manifests when a Changeling maintains the same appearance for an extended period without regularly changing shape. As the body adapts to that fixed form, the immune system begins treating the changeling's own tissues as foreign, causing chronic inflammation, morphic instability, and eventual organ failure.
The disease is biological and planar in character rather than magical. Effects that remove curses do not end it, and ordinary disease-curing magic does not cure it unless a feature says otherwise.

entries.name: Contraction and Course
entries: Chameleon's Lament is associated with identity stasis in areas where Changelings live integrated among other peoples and feel pressure to hold one form for months or years at a time. Its incubation period varies, but symptoms rarely appear before a few months of uninterrupted stasis. Once active, the disease is chronic until it is brought into remission.

entries.name: Progression
entries: Chameleon's Lament progresses through five stages, Stage I through Stage V.

items: {@b Check Frequency.} At the end of each Long Rest, the infected creature makes a Constitution saving throw against the disease.
{@b Save DC.} The save DC equals 10 + twice the disease's current stage.
{@b Failure.} Each failed save counts as 1 failure. After 3 accumulated failures, the disease advances to the next stage. A natural 1 counts as 2 failures.
{@b Success.} Each successful save counts as 1 success. After 3 accumulated successes, all accumulated successes and failures are removed. A natural 20 counts as 2 successes.

entries.name: CL Points
entries: Some stages of the disease grant CL points. CL points function like levels of {@condition Exhaustion|TGTT}, but they don't count toward the normal exhaustion cap, don't affect Constitution Saving Throws, and aren't removed by rest unless Chameleon's Lament says otherwise.

entries.name: Meaningful Shapechange
entries: To count as meaningful for treating Chameleon's Lament, a new form must differ from the previous form in at least two of the following categories:

items: Apparent species or race
Size category
Limb configuration or biological sex
Vocal structure and musculature
Facial structure

entries: Cosmetic changes such as hair color, eye color, or scars don't count.

entries.name: Stage I: Cellular Fatigue
entries: The body begins to break down, slowly but steadily. Joint pain, fatigue, and temporary bouts of weakness set in.
The infected creature gains 1 CL point.

entries.name: Stage II: Morphic Instability
entries: Internal organs begin to subtly reshape themselves and no longer fully match any one stable form. Weakness becomes more pronounced, often accompanied by short coughing fits.
The infected creature gains 1 additional CL point.

entries.name: Stage III: Systemic Rejection
entries: The immune system actively attacks the body's own tissues, causing respiratory distress, locking joints, nausea, and pain while digesting food.
The infected creature's Constitution score becomes 10, and it gains 1 additional CL point.
If the creature fails the Constitution saving throw required to assume a meaningful shapechange, it takes 16 ({@damage 3d10}) poison damage.

entries.name: Stage IV: Organ Morphosis
entries: The ability to hold a form fractures. Organs twitch and microchange uncontrollably, bloody coughing becomes common, eating is difficult, skin flakes away aggressively, and the joints swell.
The infected creature's Constitution score becomes 6.
Whenever the creature regains hit points, roll on the Organ Failure table.

entries.caption: Organ Failure
rows: The creature is {@condition blinded} for {@dice 1d4} hours.
The creature has Disadvantage on Constitution checks and saving throws and is {@condition Choked|TGTT}.
The creature gains the Sunlight Sensitivity trait for {@dice 1d4} hours.
The creature's Speed is halved, and it has Disadvantage on Dexterity saving throws.
The creature can't maintain Concentration on spells and has Disadvantage on Wisdom checks and saving throws.
The creature has Disadvantage on attack rolls because of spasms.
The creature is {@condition deafened} for {@dice 1d4} hours and has Disadvantage on Charisma checks and saving throws.
The creature has Disadvantage on Intelligence checks and saving throws because of intense pain.

entries.name: Stage V: Terminal Identity Collapse
entries: The body no longer recognizes itself. Total biological collapse is underway.
The infected creature's hit point maximum is halved.

At dawn each day, the creature makes a Constitution saving throw against the disease. On a failed save, it gains 1 level of {@condition Exhaustion|TGTT}. This exhaustion can't be removed by rest or magic while the creature remains at Stage V.
If the creature reaches 10 levels of exhaustion, its organs fail catastrophically and it dies.

entries.name: Treatment and Remission
entries: This condition can't be cured by {@spell lesser restoration|PHB}. {@spell greater restoration|PHB} reduces the disease by one stage, but it can't remove the disease entirely if the creature remains in the same form.
Spending 7 consecutive days performing a meaningful shapechange each day, with each new form held for at least 1 hour, reduces the disease by one stage.

At any stage above Stage 0, assuming a meaningful shapechange requires a Constitution saving throw against the disease's current DC. On a failure, the shapechange fails. At Stage III or higher, that failure also deals the damage described in that stage.
If the disease is reduced to Stage 0, its active symptoms end. The creature remains susceptible to recurrence if it returns to identity stasis.

entries.name: Feywild Resonance
entries: The mutable nature of the Feywild soothes Chameleon's Lament. While in the Feywild, the infected creature makes progression checks with Advantage.
Each day spent in the Feywild counts as 2 days for the purpose of therapeutic regression.

skill.name: Linguistics
entries: Study and application of languages, scripts, ciphers, and translation.
The linguistics skill is special, and gains a +1 to its modifier from each language besides common a character is proficient in

items: Identify and understand languages, dialects, and writing systems; spot forged or encoded text.
Decipher ciphers, codes, or symbolic notation; translate texts with adequate time and resources.
Recall cultural or historical context tied to specific idioms, proverbs, or phrasing.

skill.name: Culture
entries: Understanding cultures across the world—customs, laws, traditions, social cues, and etiquette.

items: Identify appropriate etiquette, taboos, or gestures in a given culture.
Recall cultural festivals, rites, or regional holidays and their significance.
Recognize heraldry, symbols, or attire linked to specific peoples or factions.

skill.name: Engineering
entries: Applying technical knowledge to structures, mechanisms, siege engines, and devices.

items: Assess structural integrity or weak points in fortifications or vehicles.
Design, repair, or sabotage mechanisms, traps, and siege engines.
Estimate load-bearing limits, bridge spans, or stress tolerances.

skill.name: Might
entries: Performing feats of raw strength: breaking stone, bending bars, lifting heavy weights, forcing doors.

items: Force open portcullises, barred doors, or heavy gates.
Bend or break metal bars, chains, or manacles.
Lift, drag, or carry exceptionally heavy objects, up to twice your normal carrying capacity.

entries.name: Carrying Capacity
entries: Your carrying capacity (in pounds) equals 50 + 25 × your Might modifier.

entries.name: Push, Drag, or Lift
entries: You can push, drag, or lift a weight in pounds up to twice your carrying capacity (100 + 50 × your Might modifier).

skill.name: Endurance
entries: Pushing your body: long marches, holding breath, resisting fatigue, poison, or drink.

items: Endure forced marches, extreme temperatures, or sleepless nights.
Hold your breath, resist drowning, or push through suffocation.
Stave off fatigue from stamina, alcohol, or prolonged strain.

feat.name: Lore Mastery
entries: When you take this feat, you gain one of the following benefits:

items: Increase your proficiency in two of your existing Lore Skills by 2 each
Gain a proficiency of +2 in two new Lore Skills you are not currently proficient in

entries: This feat can be retaken multiple times.

feat.name: Spellsword Technique
entries: You have mastered the arts of both melee combat and magical warfare, and began fusing the two into one coherent technique. You gain the following benefits:

items: You may use any simple or martial melee weapon as your spellcasting focus, as long as you are proficient in using it.
When you cast a spell of 1st level or higher as an action on your turn, you can then choose to use your bonus action to {@action dash}, {@action disengage}, or {@action dodge}.

Until the end of your next turn, If you attack with a weapon you used as a spellcasting focus, your attack deals additional {@damage 1d6} points of damage, based on the school of magic of the spell you cast.

entries.name: Damage Types
items: Evocation - The same type as that of the spell you cast, or force if the spell didn't deal damage.
Necromancy - Necrotic damage.
Abjuration - Force damage
Conjuration or Transmutation - a normal type (Bludgeoning, Piercing, and Slashing) of your choice.
Divination, Enchantment, or Illusion - Psychic damage

feat.name: Whip Master
entries: You learned how to flow through battle with grace, using your unique weapon for both combat and support. You gain the following benefits:

items: After taking the attack action with a whip on your turn, you can use your bonus action to make one melee attack with a whip. This attack uses the same ability modifier as the primary attack.

When using a whip, you can also use it to use the Grapple or Shove special melee attacks. This requires a Grapple or Shove check that work as described in the [Melee Attacks](https://www.dndbeyond.com/sources/basic-rules/combat#Grappling) section of the rules, with two major changes:
While grappling, you may not use the grappling whip to attack a target not grappled by it.
your shove may also pull the target 5ft closer to you.

Lastly, your use of your whip comes as a second nature to you. You may use your whip as an elongated appendage to perform actions that do not require fine motor skills. This includes both object interactions, such as grabbing a sword or pulling a bag towards you, and ability checks, such as an Acrobatics roll to grab a nearby support beam to swing from.

feat.name: Spell Scribing Adept

prerequisite.other: Member of the Bard, Sorcerer, or Warlock class

entries: You gain the ability to memorize a single spell from a spellbook, which you can cast using your spell slots.

Choose a class of which you are a member, from the following classes: bard, sorcerer or warlock. When you choose this feat, you acquire a spellbook holding two 1st-level spells of your choice from that class’s spell list. You can memorize one spell from your spellbook after you finish a long rest, by spending 10 minutes studying your spellbook and memorizing the incantations and gestures you must make to cast the spell. You can cast the spell you memorized using spell slots you have of the appropriate level. Your spellcasting ability for this spell is charisma.

If you come across a spell in written form, such as a magical spell scroll or a spellbook, you might be able to add it to your spellbook. The spell must be on the spell list for the class you chose, and the spell’s level can be no higher than half your levels in that class (rounded up). The process of copying the spell into your spellbook takes 2 hours per level of the spell, and costs 50 gp per level. The cost represents material components you expend as you experiment with the spell to master it, as well as the fine inks you need to record it.

feat.name: Dreamer
entries: You have an innate connection to the Dreamtime. You gain the following benefits:

items: Increase one ability score of your choice by 1, to a maximum of 20.
You learn two Dreamwalker abilities of your choice from the {@classFeature Dreamwalker Abilities|Dreamwalker|TGTT|1} list below.
{@optfeature Dreamwalk|TGTT}
{@optfeature Dreamwatch|TGTT}
{@optfeature Dreambend|TGTT}
{@optfeature Dreamjump|TGTT} ({@i Prerequisite:} {@optfeature Dreamwalk|TGTT} ability)
{@optfeature Dreamorph|TGTT} ({@i Prerequisite:} {@optfeature Dreambend|TGTT} ability)
{@optfeature Dreamforge|TGTT} ({@i Prerequisite:} {@optfeature Dreambend|TGTT} ability)
{@optfeature Dreamake|TGTT} ({@i Prerequisite:} {@optfeature Dreambend|TGTT} ability, {@optfeature Dreamforge|TGTT} ability)
{@optfeature Dreamsnatch|TGTT} ({@i Prerequisite:} {@optfeature Dreamwatch|TGTT} ability)
{@optfeature Dreamshare|TGTT} ({@i Prerequisite:} {@optfeature Dreamwatch|TGTT} ability)
{@optfeature Dreamveil|TGTT} ({@i Prerequisite:} {@optfeature Dreamwatch|TGTT} ability, {@optfeature Dreamwalk|TGTT} ability)
{@optfeature Daydream|TGTT} ({@i Prerequisite:} {@optfeature Dreamwalk|TGTT} ability)

entries: This feat can be retaken multiple times, but different Dreamwalker abilities must be chosen each time.

entries.name: Concealed
entries: A concealed weapon is easier to hide in plain sight.
You have advantage on Dexterity (Sleight of Hand) checks you make to keep this weapon {@condition hidden|tgtt}

item.name: Bullwhip
entries: A sturdy, longer version of a regular whip, which is more easily used by whip fighters in various situations that require reach.

item.name: Qilinbian
entries: Also called "Unicorn Whip", this weapon is made from steel rods decreasing in size linked by progressively smaller steel rings, enabling the user to inflict far graver damage to the enemy with quick, devastating blows.

item.name: Stockwhip
entries: Invented by cattle farmers to control their herd, this weapon is known for it's ability to produce a startling noise that draws attention.

Special - Instead of attacking, the user can attempt to crack with the whip. Every creature who can hear in a 15ft radius of the user that is concentrating on a spell must attempt a {@dc 10} Constitution Saving Throw. On failure, they lose {@status concentration}.

item.name: Smoke Bomb
entries: This alchemical creation is used for both tactical retreats and offensive methods. As an action, you can throw this bomb up to 20 feet, shattering it on impact. When shattered, it releases a smoke cloud 10 feet in diameter. The area inside the cloud is heavily obscured, and creatures beginning their turn inside the cloud or entering it who are not holding their breath beforehand must succeed on a {@dc 10} Constitution Saving Throw or become {@condition Choked|TGTT} until the start of their next turn.
The cloud lasts for 1 minute, or until a wind of moderate or greater speed (at least 10 miles per hour) disperses it.

action.name: Disruptive Strike
entries: When a creature within your melee range casts a spell, you may use your reaction to perform a disruptive strike on it. If you do, you attack the creature; on a hit, the creature must roll a concentration check, and if it fails, the casting of the spell is disrupted.

action.name: Strangle
entries: When a creature is {@condition grappled|TGTT} or {@condition restrained|TGTT}, you can use a bonus action to strangle it, provided it is within melee range. Make a melee attack; on a hit, you strangle the creature, and it becomes {@condition Choked|TGTT} and takes {@damage 1d4} bludgeoning damage. At the start of each of its turns, the creature takes {@damage 1d4} bludgeoning damage and can attempt to make a contested Strength Saving Throw or Dexterity Saving Throw against your Strength Saving Throw. It escapes your strangle on success. If the creature escapes the grapple, it automatically escapes your strangle.

action.name: Help
entries: The help action can only be performed if the character has proficiency in the skill they are trying to assist with. Helping without proficiency incurs disadvantage on the roll.

action.name: Hide
entries: You can attempt to hide when you have ¾ cover or are otherwise hidden. Make a Stealth check and note the result.
To any creature with a passive perception score lower than your check, ¾ cover grants you the {@condition hidden|TGTT} condition to them, and movement makes you {@condition Undetected|TGTT} to them.
These benefits end if:

items: You lose the {@condition hidden|TGTT} condition
You make a sound louder than a whisper
You cast a spell with a verbal component
The creature uses the {@action Search} action and succeeds against your original Stealth check
You attack
You take the Hide action again and roll lower than the creature's passive perception

entries: If you make an attack, you resolve your attack first, and lose these benefits later.
The DM might rule that other activities break your stealth, or might require another successful stealth roll to remain stealthed.

entries.name: Note
entries: When your stealth ends, you might lose the {@condition hidden|TGTT} (and {@condition undetected|TGTT}) conditions granted to you by the Hide action, but you might remain hidden for other reasons - if, for example, your stealth ends because you made a sound, but you remain behind full cover - you remain hidden.

spell.name: Goodberry
entries: Ten berries appear in your hand and are infused with magic for the duration. A creature can take a {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB} to eat one berry. Eating a berry restores 1 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point}, and the berry provides enough nourishment to sustain a creature for one day.

When a creature consumes their first berry in 24 hours, their Constitution is reduced by 2, and your max hit points are reduced by an amount equal to your level. This reduction cannot be removed before the creature eats their required food amount for a day.
Uneaten berries disappear when the spell ends.

entriesHigherLevel.name: At Higher Levels
entries: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 3rd level or higher, the berries have no ill effect on the consumer.

spell.name: Galder's Tower
entries: You conjure a two-story tower made of stone, wood, or similar suitably sturdy materials. The tower can be round or square in shape. Each level of the tower is 10 feet tall and has an area of up to 100 square feet. Access between levels consists of a simple ladder and hatch. Each level takes one of the following forms, chosen by you when you cast the spell:

items: A bedroom with a bed, chairs, chest, and magical fireplace
A study with desks, books, bookshelves, parchments, ink, and ink pens
A dining space with a table, chairs, magical fireplace, containers, and cooking utensils
A lounge with couches, armchairs, side tables and footstools
A washroom with toilets, washtubs, a magical brazier, and sauna benches
An observatory with a telescope and maps of the night sky
An unfurnished, empty room

entries: The interior of the tower is warm and dry, regardless of conditions outside. Any equipment or furnishings conjured with the tower dissipate into smoke if removed from it. At the end of the spell's duration, all creatures and objects within the tower that were not created by the spell appear safely outside on the ground, and all traces of the tower and its furnishings disappear.

You can cast this spell again while it is active to maintain the tower's existence for another 24 hours. You can create a permanent tower by casting this spell in the same location and with the same configuration every day for one year.
{@b Note} - When you cast this spell as part of a Journey or Camp, you gain a +2 to your Risk Modifier.

spell.name: Leomund’s Tiny Hut
entries: A 10-foot {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation} springs into existence around you and remains stationary for the duration. The spell fails when you cast it if the {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation} isn't big enough to fully encapsulate all creatures in its area.

Creatures and objects within the {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation} when you cast the spell can move through it freely. All other creatures and objects are barred from passing through it. Spells of level 3 or lower can't be cast through it, and the effects of such spells can't extend into it.

The atmosphere inside the {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation} is comfortable and dry, regardless of the weather outside. Until the spell ends, you can command the interior to have {@variantrule Dim Light|XPHB} or {@variantrule Darkness|XPHB} (no action required). The {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation} is opaque from the outside and of any color you choose, but it's transparent from the inside.
The spell ends early if you leave the {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation} or if you cast it again.
{@b Note} - When you cast this spell as part of a Journey or Camp, you gain a +1 to your Risk Modifier.

spell.name: Daylight
entries: For the duration, sunlight spreads from a point within range and fills a 60-foot-radius {@variantrule Sphere [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Sphere}. The sunlight's area is {@variantrule Bright Light|XPHB} and sheds {@variantrule Dim Light|XPHB} for an additional 60 feet.

Alternatively, you cast the spell on an object that isn't being worn or carried, causing the sunlight to fill a 60-foot {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation} originating from that object. Covering that object with something opaque, such as a bowl or helm, blocks the sunlight.
If any of this spell's area overlaps with an area of {@variantrule Darkness|XPHB} created by a spell of level 3 or lower, that other spell is dispelled.
{@b Note} - When you cast this spell as part of a Journey or Camp when there is not outside light source, you gain a +3 to your Risk Modifier.

spell.name: Nox
entries: Artificial sources of light in the range of the spell become dimmer, shedding light 1 level of brightness less than before (bright light becomes dim light, dim light becomes darkness).

spell.name: Amanuensis

m.text: an inkwell worth at least 10 gp

entries: You manipulate the written word in a variety of ways. You can create one of the following effects within range:

items: You cause nonmagical text (up to 250 words) from one source to be copied into a book, paper, or parchment for 1 minute. Multiple castings can continue this effect for as long as you have available material.

You create a magical sensor in the air that records all speech within 30 feet in a book, paper, or parchment for 1 minute. Multiple castings can continue this effect for as long as you have available material.
You touch an item containing some form of text and speak a word or phrase. For 1 minute, all references to that word or phrase glow luminously where present on or in the item.

You graffiti text onto any surface, using the ink from the material component. The message can be up to 25 words long and 10 feet on a side, in any language and style you know, and lasts until worn or washed away. This expends the material component.
You create a single illustration on a page or parchment, limited to the color of the ink used in the material component and your own artistic ability.

entries: If you cast this spell multiple times, you can have up to three of its effects active at a time, and you can dismiss such an effect with an action.

spell.name: Transposition
entries: As an action, you can teleport in a straight line to a point you can see up to 20 feet away from you.

Any creature standing between you and your destination must make a Strength Saving Throw. On a failed save, the creature takes {@scaledamage 1d6|1-4,5-10,11-16,17-20|1d6,2d6,3d6,4d6} force damage. On a successful save, your spell gets disrupted and you immediately appear next to the creature who blocked your path.

This spell's damage increases by {@scaledice 1d6|5,11,17|1d6} when you reach 5th level ({@scaledice 2d6|5-10|2d6}), 11th level ({@scaledice 3d6|11-16|3d6}), and 17th level ({@scaledice 4d6|17-20|4d6}).

Alternatively, you can use this cantrip to move an object you touch the same way, or call an object you see to your hand. The objects will not damage creatures in their path but might still get stuck if the creatures succeed in their Strength Saving Throw.

spell.name: Adhesion

m.text: a pinch of powdered horse's hoof

entries: This spell causes two solid objects to adhere to one another for the duration of the spell. The caster must touch the juncture between the objects while intoning the spell. The adhered objects can be separated by a successful Athletics check against the caster's spell save DC, by means of a {@spell dispel magic|TGTT} spell, or by exerting a force of more than 1,000 pounds plus 100 pounds per caster level.
If the spell is employed against materials or objects worn or carried by an unwilling creature, the subject gains a Strength Saving Throw to avoid the effect.

spell.name: Amplify
entries: This spell changes the volume of sounds created close to its center of effect. The caster moves their hand in a circular motion at the point they want to center the spell. With the uttering of a command word, the area outlined begins to glow softly. The outlined area is disk shaped and can be as small as 2 inches in diameter or as large as 1 foot in diameter.

The disk acts as a sound magnifier: Whatever sound enters the active (caster) side of the disk can exit the other side at a much different volume. The volume can be increased or decreased at the caster's mental command: up to five times louder, or softer, than normal.
The spell has no effect on special sound-based attacks.

spell.name: Chameleon

m.text: a shed skin of a small lizard

entries: This spell alters the coloration of a creature to match the surrounding background. The creature is always hiding while the spell is active, and attempting to locate the creature requires a {@skill Perception} check at disadvantage.
If the creature attacks or casts a spell, this spell ends.

spell.name: Comprehend Languages

components.m: a pinch of soot and salt

entries: For the duration, you gain a general understanding of any spoken language you hear around you. You do not understand the actual words and their meanings, only gain a vague understanding of the meaning and tone of the speaker.
This spell doesn't decode secret messages in speech, such as Thieves' Cant.
Alternatively, this spell can give a +10 to any linguistics skill roll made by the caster for the duration

spell.name: Copy

components.m: blank parchment or blank pages of sufficient size to hold the copy

entries: The spell enables the caster to make a perfect copy of a map, letter, or other written or drawn document.

The caster holds the blank object over the object to be copied, then casts the spell; the copy appears on the formerly blank object. The length of the text is limited to 10 pages of standard size (roughly 9 inches by 12 inches).

spell.name: Detect Magic
entries: For the duration, you sense the presence of magic within 30 feet of you. If you sense magic in this way, you can choose to see a faint aura around any visible creature or object in the area that bears magic, and you learn its school of magic, if any.
The spell can penetrate most barriers, but it is blocked by 1 foot of stone, 1 inch of common metal, a thin sheet of lead, or 3 feet of wood or dirt.

entriesHigherLevel.name: At Higher Levels
entries: When casting the spell with a {@b 2nd} level slot, the caster can deduce the strength of the spell, though not accurately, based on the behavior of the aura. This follows the guidelines:

rows: A thin, unmoving glow around the object.
A dense hue around the object that fades after an inch or two
A slow and transparent molten aura that ends cleanly and without fading
A beating aura, like a heart.
An almost blinding radiance of color that swirls around itself.

entries: Some creatures also have a magical aura. In their case, the aura's strength will correlate with the most powerful spells they can cast.

Furthermore, when casting a spell this way or with a higher level spell slot, you are able to see magical residue. Magic sometimes leaves signs of its presence; The residual energy fades with time, and so more powerful spells can be detected for longer periods of time.
When casting the spell with a {@b 3rd} level slot, information about the source of the magic can be gleaned:

rows: The aura will have white glow to it, regardless of other colors
The aura will have a black unglow to it, regardless of other colors
The aura will have grey undertones
The aura will appear scale-like, and will have tinges of color that represent the dragon caster
The aura will take on properties of the plane from which it hails (flow like water, look like flames, become solid like rock, etc)
The aura will look sickly, parts of it barely moving, parts lashing violently at each other and dissipating. It will give a strong sensation of destructiveness.

Some magic comes from the world itself, i.e druids, wild magic sorcerers, etc. This magic varies greatly between origins. Druidic magic will have a green and brown glow to it, while wild magic will appear to interfere with the color of the school of magic, creating ripples of rainbow colors in it.
The aura will have faint echoes of overlapping auras
The aura will pulse with colors traveling around it, like a neural network
Monks use a type of magical energy that is unique to them and utilizes the energy of their very soul. This creates a golden glow to the aura, regardless of other colors
The aura's colors will be muted
The aura's colors will be extremely Vibrant
The aura will change shapes, and most shapes will be impossible and unnatural

entries: A spell can have multiple sources in some cases (for example, a cleric casting a spell is fueled by a celestial being) and will simply have the features of all relevant sources.

When casting the spell with a {@b 4th} level slot, you may be able to recognize the specific "fingerprint" of the creature that cast the magic. This fingerprint is a look and substance that the aura has that is unique to an individual, and therefore can be used to determine if two spells were cast by the same creature.
Once You have identified a creature's aura on at least two spells, you know it for purposes of other spells that may interact with it (i.e {@spell scrying|TGTT}).

spell.name: Identify

components.m: a pearl worth at least 100 gp and an owl feather

entries: You choose one object that you must touch throughout the casting of the spell. If it is a magic item or some other magic-imbued object, you get a +10 bonus to the {@skill Arcana} check for identifying its magical effects.
You also get knowledge of the exact number of charges and of the command word (assuming any of these are relevant to the item).
The spell prevents false information from being gleaned about the item if you fail on your check.
If you instead touch a creature throughout the casting, you learn what spells, if any, are currently affecting it.

entriesHigherLevel.name: At Higher Levels
entries: When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 2nd level or higher, the bonus increases by +1 for each slot level above 1st.
When you cast this spell using a spell slot of 3rd level or higher, if you succeed in getting a roll of 5 more than the items DC, you may learn about curses the item bears.

spell.name: Ventriloquism
entries: This spell enables the caster to make it sound as if their voice or someone's voice or similar sound is issuing from someplace else, such as from another creature, a statue, from behind a door, down a passage, etc.

The spell caster is able to make their voice sound as if a different creature were speaking or making the noise; of course, in a language known by the caster, or a sound which the caster can normally make.

spell.name: Wall of Darkness

components.m: a silver ring worth at least 25 gp

entries: This spell brings into being a 10 foot wide, 60 foot long and 20 foot tall wall of blackness which cannot be seen through, even with {@sense darkvision}. It is dispelled instantly by any light generating spell of first level or higher. It has no physical existence, and does not hinder nor harm those passing through it.
